I have, I'll try to remember to send you some of the old theft control stuff. I have a little file. Is he still in business? No, Jack was a really interesting story. Jack, he made two, well he's an ex-deliverman thief. See that was the story. So he's a bread truck guy, Pepsi truck guy, and so he knew how they stole because he stole. And he was a real, real, real, real evangelical 666, the world's coming to an end, you know, that kind of thing. And so Jack, the year I got him, he made 200 grand. The only product he had was six cassette tapes with a rubber band around him. He was selling them for 10 bucks in the back of the seminar. And he's working for 500 bucks a day for his basic seminar. First thing I did was raise the price from 500 to 5,000. We lost one client. Jack tells you what the value was. But he did almost $11 million the third year and went nuts. And I do mean nuts. He left wife, family, business, everything, no notice, left a note on the desk. He was going off to save the world from the impending apocalypse and became a self-ordained minister in the hills of Tennessee or Appalachia or someplace and nobody's ever heard from him since. His private pilot, because he flew to the seminars and we loaded this plane with probably a pad. There was room for Pat and Jack and then Product, you know, when they left at the first of the week. And Pat always used to say, I'm not sure we can get off the ground. Yeah, you can get it. Go get it. But Pat called me and said, Jack's gone. I said, what do you mean Jack's gone? He said, let me read you the note. He said, Jack's gone. You know, we got five seminars this week. What should I do? I said, go do them, bud. But never heard from him again. Wife never heard from him. Nobody ever heard from him. Is the business done? Well, it was done as soon as he was done. So that's something you started up? Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah. Now, technology has improved a lot. So a lot of what we taught has actually been solved, like at the supermarket level. Technology's better. Sure. But he was very charismatic personality. He was great in front of the room. And we got real good at selling a lot of stuff. I mean, I had to train the trainer system that he sold with DVDs and stuff for $15,000. And you still have all that? I have pieces and parts. And yeah. But I can do the seminars. That I can do. Yeah. How do you know he doesn't have one? The things you've done. I'm going to tell you what, if we had him do a background check on everybody coming to our seminars, you and I'd want personal security. Casey. Casey. There you go. So what do you know you didn't know when you came in? Well, as you know, Dan, I'm the one-eyed man. You know, in the land of the Blonde. And I guess now that my other eye has been opened, I'll be virtually unstoppable, I think. I guess, you know, I was trying to do it and, you know, doing it okay. But I think doing it without fully understanding, you know, the mechanics and the things I should be really trying to hit each time. And I suppose, you know, making a distinction between advocacy and how to and where you're demonstrating credibility and where you want to influence. And in fact, if you've got time to write a story, I remember reading an article of yours a bit over 18 months ago now, I guess. You had an article published in Michael Masterson's EZ, Healthy Wealthy and Wise. And it started out, you know, if you lost your fortune and had to start again, where would you start? What sort of business? And you said the newsletter business. And you were talking about specialisation. So I thought I could do that. And so I started actually a marketing letter for, you know, hydraulic shops and distributors. So, you know, branching out from, you're talking about moving horizontally or up, because it's a move up the food chain in terms of product price point, etc. And like a good entrepreneur, I went off half copped, you know, but I did it. And I ran it for about 12 months. And I had abandoned it by planning to go back. And I think one of the things that just hit me, well, today really, was that in that marketing letter, you know, many mistakes were made, but I was trying to teach. And I realised that it was a really hard road to have. How much money do you want to set out to explain marketing to non-marketing people who don't even know they should be doing it? You know, you've got to really kind of heat them up to that before they're ready to hear about unique selling proposition or headlines or, you know, because you're probably starting with those industrial guys where they don't even know they should be doing it. And there's language sensitivities. I mean, like in a profession, I joke about it, but I watch it at the same time. You know, like you start using words like sale and close, and they're like, they just turn you off for the most part. So there's language sensitivities. The one thing I would say to you about your emails, and I pulled an example. No, I'm not on this email list, you asshole. Not on anybody's email list.
